questions.question: Define Asymptotic Analysis and explain why it is important in algorithm design.
keyPoints: Evaluates algorithm efficiency as input size approaches infinity
Ignores constant factors and lower-order terms
Allows comparison of algorithms independent of hardware
Provides mathematical framework for performance prediction
Enables selection of appropriate algorithm for problem size

questions.question: Explain the difference between Big O, Big Omega, and Big Theta notations with examples.
keyPoints: Big O (O): Upper bound - worst case scenario
Big Omega (Ω): Lower bound - best case scenario
Big Theta (Θ): Tight bound - both upper and lower
Example: Search in sorted array - O(n), Ω(1), Θ doesn't exist for all cases
Θ exists when upper and lower bounds are same (e.g., array access is Θ(1))

questions.question: Arrange the following complexities in increasing order and briefly explain: O(1), O(n²), O(n!), O(log n), O(n log n), O(2^n), O(n)
keyPoints: Correct order: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²) < O(2^n) < O(n!)
O(1): Constant - same time regardless of size
O(log n): Logarithmic - halves problem each step
O(n): Linear - time proportional to input
O(n log n): Linearithmic - common in efficient sorting
O(n²): Quadratic - nested loops
O(2^n) and O(n!): Exponential/Factorial - impractical for large n

questions.question: What is the Master Theorem? Explain its three cases with the conditions for each.
keyPoints: Provides solution for recurrence T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)
Case 1: If f(n) = O(n^(log_b a - ε)), then T(n) = Θ(n^(log_b a))
Case 2: If f(n) = Θ(n^(log_b a)), then T(n) = Θ(n^(log_b a) log n)
Case 3: If f(n) = Ω(n^(log_b a + ε)) and regularity condition holds, then T(n) = Θ(f(n))
Used for divide-and-conquer algorithms like Merge Sort, Binary Search

questions.question: Differentiate between time complexity and space complexity. Also explain what is meant by auxiliary space.
keyPoints: Time complexity: Amount of time an algorithm needs to complete
Space complexity: Amount of memory an algorithm requires
Auxiliary space: Extra space used by algorithm excluding input
In-place algorithms use O(1) auxiliary space
Example: Merge Sort has O(n) time and O(n) auxiliary space; Quick Sort has O(n log n) average time and O(log n) auxiliary space

questions.question: What is Amortized Analysis? Give an example of when it is used.
keyPoints: Analyzes average cost per operation over worst-case sequence of operations
Different from average-case analysis which assumes random input
Used for data structures with occasional expensive operations but cheap average cost
Example: Dynamic Array - occasional doubling is expensive but amortized O(1) for insertion
Used in: Dynamic arrays, Union-Find data structure, Splay trees

questions.question: Solve the following recurrence relations using Master Theorem or recursion tree method: (a) T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n (b) T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n² (c) T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1)
keyPoints: (a) a=2, b=2, f(n)=n. n^(log₂2) = n. Since f(n) = Θ(n), Case 2 applies. T(n) = Θ(n log n)
(b) a=4, b=2, f(n)=n². n^(log₂4) = n². Since f(n) = Θ(n²), Case 2 applies. T(n) = Θ(n² log n)
(c) a=1, b=2, f(n)=O(1). n^(log₂1) = n⁰ = 1. Since f(n) = Θ(1), Case 2 applies. T(n) = Θ(log n)
Alternatively for (c): Recursion tree has log n levels, each with O(1) work = O(log n)

questions.question: Explain what is meant by 'best case', 'average case', and 'worst case' complexity. Analyze all three cases for Linear Search and Binary Search algorithms.
keyPoints: Best case: Minimum time for favorable input; Average case: Expected time over all possible inputs; Worst case: Maximum time for worst input
Linear Search: Best=O(1) - element at first position; Average=O(n) - element equally likely anywhere; Worst=O(n) - element at end or not present
Binary Search: Best=O(1) - element at middle; Average=O(log n); Worst=O(log n) - element at extreme or not present
For Binary Search, best and worst cases differ only by constant factor; Average case assumes uniform distribution of target
Practical significance: Worst case determines algorithm's reliability guarantee

questions.question: Discuss the following time complexities in detail with one example algorithm for each: O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n²), O(2^n). Also mention which ones are considered 'practical' and why.
keyPoints: O(1): Constant - array access, hash table lookup
O(log n): Logarithmic - binary search, balanced tree operations
O(n): Linear - linear search, counting sort
O(n log n): Linearithmic - merge sort, heap sort
O(n²): Quadratic - bubble sort, nested loops
O(2^n): Exponential - recursive Fibonacci, subset generation
Practical: O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n) - usable for large inputs
Impractical: O(n²), O(2^n), O(n!) - grow too fast for large n
Selection depends on input size: for n<100, O(n²) may be acceptable; for n>10⁶, need O(n log n) or better

questions.question: Write detailed notes on: (a) Recurrence Relations and their importance (b) Substitution Method for solving recurrences (c) Iteration Method for solving recurrences
keyPoints: (a) Recurrence Relations: Mathematical representation of algorithm's time complexity in terms of smaller inputs; essential for divide-and-conquer algorithms; examples: T(n)=T(n/2)+O(1), T(n)=2T(n/2)+O(n)
(b) Substitution Method: Guess the solution and prove by induction; two parts: guessing form and verifying by substitution; useful for establishing upper/lower bounds

(c) Iteration Method: Expand recurrence repeatedly to convert to summation; count number of iterations and work per iteration; then evaluate summation to get complexity; useful when Master Theorem doesn't apply
All three methods aim to solve T(n) in closed form for complexity analysis
